1: "Brothers and sisters, if someone is caught in a sin, you who live by the Spirit should restore that person gently" (oi pneumatikoi katartizete ton toiouton en pneumati praytētos) — katartizō (to restore, repair, mend, put right) is a medical term for setting a broken bone and a fishing term for mending nets (Matthew 4:21). The community has a responsibility to restore the fallen with the same care that a physician or craftsman applies to repairing something broken — gently and with expertise. Katartizete (present active imperative: keep restoring, be in the practice of restoring) indicates this is an ongoing community function, not a one-time emergency response.
2: "Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ" (allēlōn ta barē bastazete kai houtōs anaplērōsete ton nomon tou Christou) — baros (burden, weight) — the physical weight of a heavy load; the community's mutual burden-bearing is the practical expression of love that fulfills "the law of Christ" (the love-command of John 13:34-35; Galatians 15:12). The "law of Christ" is both a reference to Jesus's specific commandments and to the entire pattern of his self-giving life as the regulative principle for community ethics. The contrast with the "law of Moses" being argued throughout the letter is pointed: the law that truly matters for the new-covenant community is the law of Christ.
3: "If anyone thinks they are something when they are not, they deceive themselves" (ei gar dokei tis einai ti mēden ōn, phrenapata heauton) — phrenapataō (to deceive the mind, delude oneself) — a compound found only here in the NT. The self-deception of the person who overestimates their spiritual status is the root of the failure to bear others' burdens: the one who considers themselves exceptional does not need to share the common human load. The Galatian agitators who consider themselves spiritually superior (having the full law-keeping that the Gentile converts lack) exemplify this self-deception.
4: "Each one should test their own actions. Then they can take pride in themselves alone, without comparing themselves to someone else" (to de ergon heautou dokimazeto hekastos, kai tote eis heauton monon to kauchēma exei kai ouk eis ton heteron) — dokimazō (to test, examine, prove) — the same testing/approval language as Romans 12:2 and 1 Corinthians 11:28; self-examination rather than competitive self-comparison is the appropriate mode of moral assessment. The boast (kauchēma) that comes from genuine self-examination is not comparative pride but the legitimate satisfaction of genuine work done well.
5: "For each one should carry their own load" (hekastos gar to idion phortion bastasei) — phortion (pack, load, burden) is a different word from baros (v2 — the excess weight): phortion is the normal load that a person carries as their own responsibility (a soldier's pack, a traveler's bag). Paul is not contradicting v2 but distinguishing: the community bears one another's excess burdens while each person carries their own normal load of responsibility. The eschatological note in the future tense (bastasei = will carry) may hint at the last judgment at which each will account for their own actions (v5 in context of vv4-5).
6: "Nevertheless, the one who receives instruction in the word should share all good things with their instructor" (koinōneitō de ho katēchoumenos ton logon tō katēchounti en pasin agathois) — katēchoumai (to be taught, receive oral instruction — the root of "catechism") and katēcheō (to instruct, teach) describe the formal teacher-learner relationship. The instruction in the word was likely done through a teacher designated for this purpose in the community. The financial support of teachers was not universal in ancient culture: Stoic philosophers often taught freely, while Sophists charged fees. The early church developed its own norms for teacher-support, reflected here and in 1 Corinthians 9:14; 1 Timothy 5:17-18.
7: "Do not be deceived: God cannot be mocked. A man reaps what he sows" (mē planasthe, theos ou myktērizetai; ho gar ean speirē anthrōpos, touto kai therisei) — myktērizō (to turn up the nose at, mock, treat with contempt) — a hapax legomenon in the NT. The agricultural proverb of sowing and reaping appears in widely distributed ancient literature (Job 4:8; Proverbs 22:8; Hosea 8:7; 2 Corinthians 9:6) but is here applied specifically to the moral-spiritual domain: what one sows to (flesh or Spirit) determines what kind of harvest one will reap. The proverb grounds the coming warning about the eternal consequences of lifestyle orientation.
8: "Whoever sows to please their flesh, from the flesh will reap destruction; whoever sows to please the Spirit, from the Spirit will reap eternal life" (ho speirōn eis tēn sarka heautou ek tēs sarkos therisei phthoran; ho de speirōn eis to pneuma ek tou pneumatos therisei zōēn aiōnion) — phthora (destruction, decay, corruption) and zōē aiōnios (eternal life) are the eschatological harvest of the two different sowings. The present tenses of sowing (speirōn) and the future tense of reaping (therisei) maintain the temporal structure: the sowing is present; the harvest is future. The flesh-to-corruption connection reflects the Adamic mortality that the flesh-orientation perpetuates; the Spirit-to-life connection reflects the resurrection-life that the Spirit inaugurates.
9: "Let us not become weary in doing good, for at the proper time we will reap a harvest if we do not give up" (to de kalon poiountes mē ekkakōmen; kairō gar idiō therisomen mē eklyomenoi) — ekkakeō (to grow weary, lose heart) and eklyō (to be exhausted, lose strength) describe the fatigue of sustained ethical effort. The "proper time" (kairō idiō) is the eschatological moment of harvest — the final judgment at which the sowing of good deeds will be rewarded. The patience required to wait for the harvest without giving up is the same patience (hypomonē) that Paul commends elsewhere in contexts of eschatological hope (Romans 5:3-4; Galatians 8:25).
10: "Therefore, as we have opportunity, let us do good to all people, especially to those who belong to the family of believers" (hōs kairon echomen, ergazōmetha to agathon pros pantas, malista de pros tous oikeious tēs pisteōs) — oikeious tēs pisteōs (household of faith, domestic members of the faith) — the community as a household (oikos) defined by shared faith rather than blood kinship. The priority of the faith-household in community care does not exclude outsiders but establishes the primary community of obligation while extending good to all. The "as we have opportunity" (hōs kairon echomen) introduces the kairos-concept of appropriate, well-timed action.
11: "See what large letters I use as I write to you with my own hand!" (idete pēlikois hymin grammasin egrapsa tē emē cheiri) — the size of Paul's writing (pēlikois grammasin) has been debated: (a) a sign of genuine physical eye-trouble that made small writing difficult (connecting to Galatians 4:15); (b) the ordinary "large hand" of someone not professionally trained as a scribe; (c) a deliberate emphasis marking the personal importance of the concluding section. Letters in antiquity were typically dictated to an amanuensis (secretary) and then the author would add a closing greeting in their own hand to authenticate the letter (cf. 1 Corinthians 16:21; Colossians 4:18; 2 Thessalonians 3:17).
12: "Those who want to impress people by means of the flesh are trying to compel you to be circumcised. The only reason they do this is to avoid being persecuted for the cross of Christ" (hosoi theloustin euprosōpēsai en sarki, houtoi anankamzousin hymas peritemnesasthai, monon hina tō staurō tou Christou mē diōkōntai) — euprosōpeō (to make a good show, have a good public face) — the social motivation: the agitators were avoiding persecution from Jewish communities by demonstrating that their Gentile converts were law-observant. A circumcised Gentile community was safe within the Jewish communal structure; an uncircumcised Gentile community following a crucified messianic pretender was religiously and socially exposed.
13: "Not even those who are circumcised keep the law, yet they want you to be circumcised that they may boast about your flesh" (oude gar hoi peritemnnomenoi autoi nomon phylassousin, alla theloustin hymas peritemnesasthai hina en tē hymetera sarki kauchēsōntai) — Paul's charge that the circumcisers do not themselves fully keep the law echoes his criticism of Jewish hypocrisy in Romans 2:17-24: claiming law-devotion while not practicing it. The motive he identifies — boasting in the Galatians' flesh (their circumcision as the agitators' achievement) — is the social prestige of a successful circumcision-campaign among formerly uncircumcised Gentile communities.
14: "May I never boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, through which the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world" (emoi de mē genoito kauchasthai ei mē en tō staurō tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou, di' hou emoi kosmos estaurōtai kagō kosmō) — Paul's counter-boast against the agitators' boasting in circumcision: his only boast is in the instrument of his Lord's shameful death. The cross as the object of boasting was categorically shocking in the Roman world, where crucifixion was the most degrading form of execution reserved for slaves and criminals. The mutual crucifixion of Paul to the world and the world to Paul describes the radical social dislocation that results from the cross becoming one's primary allegiance.
15: "Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision means anything; what counts is the new creation" (oute gar peritomē ti estin oute akrobystia, alla kainē ktisis) — the declaration that the primary religious distinction of the ancient world (Jew/Gentile, circumcised/uncircumcised) is relativized by the category of "new creation" (kainē ktisis) picks up the eschatological theology of 2 Corinthians 5:17 ("if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come"). The kainē ktisis is not merely personal renewal but the eschatological new creation of Isaiah 65:17-25 and 66:22 already inaugurated in the Christ-event. Circumcision and uncircumcision belong to the old creation that is passing away.
16: "Peace and mercy to all who follow this rule — to the Israel of God" (kai hosoi tō kanoni toutō stoichēsousin, eirēnē ep' autous kai eleos, kai epi ton Israēl tou theou) — "the Israel of God" (tou Israēl tou theou) is one of the most debated phrases in Galatians. Options: (a) the whole church (Jewish and Gentile believers) = Israel of God (common Protestant interpretation); (b) Jewish believers specifically — the believing remnant within ethnic Israel; (c) future ethnic Israel whose salvation Paul hopes for (cf. Romans 11:26). The grammar (kai epi can be "and also" or simply "and") determines whether this is one group (the kai is epexegetical) or two (all who follow this rule, and also Israel of God).
17: "From now on, let no one cause me trouble, for I bear on my body the marks of Jesus" (ta stigmata tou Iēsou en tō sōmati mou bastazō) — stigmata (marks, brands) — in the Greco-Roman world, slaves were branded with their owner's mark to identify them as property; devotees of certain mystery cults were tattooed to mark their religious allegiance; soldiers bore identification marks. Paul's stigmata tou Iēsou are the physical marks left by his sufferings for the gospel: beatings, stonings, imprisonments (2 Corinthians 11:23-27). These marks on his body are his counter-claim to the circumcision the agitators promote: circumcision marks the flesh with the sign of one master's law; Paul's scars mark him as belonging to Jesus through suffering.
18: "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers and sisters. Amen." — the closing benediction is the shortest of Paul's epistolary conclusions and notably includes "brothers and sisters" (adelphoi) — the family language with which Paul began his emotional appeal in Galatians 4:12. Despite the sharp polemic of the letter, Paul closes by claiming the Galatians as his siblings in Christ. The grace-benediction circles back to the opening (Galatians 1:3: grace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ) and to the thesis (Galatians 2:21: I do not nullify the grace of God): grace is the beginning, the subject, and the ending of the letter's argument.
